speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 13, 1996. The survivor is Louis Hofman-- L-O-U-I-S, H-O-F-M-A-N. The interviewer is Eugenia Rosen, R-O-S-E-N. We are in North Miami Beach in the Miami Florida District, USA. The language will be English and  Yiddish, and this is roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Eugenia Rosen. Today's date is May 13, 1996. And I'm conducting an interview with survivor Louis Hofman in North Miami Beach, Florida, United States of America. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Louis Hofman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. L-O-U-I-S. Hofman, H-O-F-M-A-N. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When are you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in January the 12 of 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm today 84.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in-- in Konstantyn a small town not far from the River Bug. My father's name was-- my Jewish name is [INAUDIBLE]. Mother's name is [INAUDIBLE]. I was born in Konstantyn. And I was raised there till the Germans destroyed the town and the-- and the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near what large city is Konstantynow?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Near what large--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is near Biala Podlaska. Biala Podlaska was the state of our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other city is it near, a larger city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Biala Podlaska is a large city. It was about-- about 15,000, 20,000-- between 15,000 and 20,000 Jews. No, no-- people. 8,000 Jewish people. And near it was another city, a big one, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What rivers are near this city? What rivers are near Konstantynow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The river?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the River Bug. Bug-- B-U-G-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had four brothers and four sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names, yes. I'm going from the scratch, from the beginning. My brother's name was Elie, Elie. And his wife was [? Shendel. ?] They had one daughter, married. She-- the name was Esther Silverman. Her husband's name was Abraham. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then my-- then the-- the second one was my sister Sara. Her husband's name was [? Schloymeh. ?] They had four children. The old-- the oldest son was [? Ella. ?] The daughter's name was [? Chana. ?] And the other two, [? Moishe ?] and [? Yankashia. ?] Next was my sister's name, Chaya Rosenbaum. My brother-in-law's name was Chaim. The children's name was [? Chana, ?] the daughter, and [? Moyshabel, ?] and-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [? Moyshabel ?] and the other, [INAUDIBLE]. Three more, but I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your other sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- now-- now comes my sister-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Nechama-- Nechama. It's Goldring. The husband's name was [? Yoshia. ?] And the daughter was [? Connie Etel, ?] Esther, and [? Tile ?] Goldring. Now I'm going to my brother after this. Yankev Hofman. His wife's name was [? Shana ?]. It was [? Shana-- ?] wait, wait, wait. It was [? Angela, ?] [? Wohala, ?] and [INAUDIBLE], and-- and [? Fimala. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Another brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another-- there comes a sister, Brucha Walecki. Her husband's name was Eli, Eli. And the-- she-- they had four children. It was [? Chavalla ?], [INAUDIBLE], and [? Boyshele, ?] and the-- the last one I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: The last brother's name was Enoch Hofman. His wife was [? Sura. ?] They didn't have children, because they both just got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father? Can you tell me his name and something about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Gedale Hofman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Hasid? Was he a Hasid? A Hasid?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Hasidic Jew from the dynasty [? Radzim. ?] There was a famous Hasidic culture place in the [INAUDIBLE]. And my father was-- was joined the Hasidic people from [? Radzim. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he go to see the rabbi from [? Radzim? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still remember the names of the rabbis. One was [INAUDIBLE]. And [INAUDIBLE]. And the last one was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [? Radzim, ?] Poland. Not far from Warsaw, from [INAUDIBLE], in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did he go to see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father used to go a lot of times for Rosh Hashanah there. But I never went there, because I was about-- small child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers or sisters? Did they go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: To [? Radzim? ?] No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only your father went?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me some--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was expensive to go there. It was far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what your father did? How did he support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, when he got married, he was Hasidic. He was, but my grandpa [INAUDIBLE]. But later, my mother was-- my grandpa bought a butcher shop, a kosher butcher shop. My mother, she knew how to [INAUDIBLE] and how to sell meat to the-- to the housewives. Then my father was for a short time with them, and then he was-- he went on his own. He bought a [? car, ?] like in a small town. He brought to the slaughter house, and they-- there was people and they took-- they took care, and they brought us into the store. And my mother was [INAUDIBLE] sell the meat. And he was more or less on the-- on the bookkeeping, what they used to take-- take on credit, and so far. My father was never selling meat. My poor mother was working very, very hard in her life-- very hard. She had to raise eight children and stay in-- in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where the store was, on what street?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the main-- main-- on the main street in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Konstantynow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Konstantyn. The number from the store, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if your mother wore a sheitel?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: A sheitel.

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to wear a sheitel. She used to go [INAUDIBLE]. And she used to serve always for poor people. She-- but mine-- the table was always with somebody, poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos, and even in the middle of the week, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. And tell me about Shabbos, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, she used to work very hard. But she prepared everything for Shabbos, a nice Shabbos meal with the children together. They were singing [INAUDIBLE]. And we were very respectable. Treated by the parents, and we was very respect the parents. It was a very close family. The fact is, a lot of people they were jealous because [INAUDIBLE] Hofman is a nice family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kind of special foods did your mother cook on Shabbos, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, for-- for Shabbos, they put in a cholent, Friday. But otherwise, they cooked-- she cooked [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she put in the cholent? At home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bakery, not far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went to bring the cholent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to bring, because it was not allowed to carry on Shabbos for grown-up people. The [INAUDIBLE] was not completed. Then my brother used to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you bring the cholent?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the [INAUDIBLE] [? shul. ?] After we came back from the services.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people-- how many Jewish people lived in Konstantynow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 1,500 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were all Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was all kind, like in any other small city, small towns. It was all kinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many synagogues were there? How many synagogues?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a nice beautiful synagogue, very-- a nice one. Very-- And there was a big [INAUDIBLE]. It was a few [INAUDIBLE] went they went to daven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rabbi's name from this synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbis I remember is-- his name was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the synagogue that you attended? Is this the synagogue that you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was-- in a few of-- mixed. They-- [INAUDIBLE] The rabbi was not a teacher for the children. [SPEAKING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they come for Shabbos and stay over?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in for the-- they went separate, but-- but [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And for the holidays, did they come?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also they went, but separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which was one of your favorite holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the holidays was favorite, because was-- everything was-- Pesach was the most popular holiday. But together, by the Seder, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- what kind-- what language was spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mamaloshen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mamaloshen.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the children spoke Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the relationship between the non-Jews and Jews in the city, when there were the holidays and when you were going to shul? Was there any incidents? Tell me about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not so bad, but was no good. They was-- one thing of the Jews, Yiddish, this and that. But it was not too bad. Anti-Semitic was at that time also.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. In my-- in my hometown even, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very small. One bedroom, one living room, and one kitchen. We used to sleep two, three people in one bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the store? The butcher shop, where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far away. About half a block. It was a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your school? What school did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the Polish school. Seven grades. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], Polish [INAUDIBLE] School, and then I went to cheder. And I went to a private teacher for writing and reading Yiddish. And the [INAUDIBLE]-- And this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had to help the parents to make a living. We couldn't do any other things. We got this-- after the bar mitzvah, I had to help out already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your bar mitzvah? Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- a very poor bar mitzvah. I went to the [INAUDIBLE] to the Sefer Torah, to make the barucha, and say the haftarah. And my father, rest in peace, he brought a bottle of whiskey and a piece of cake and we drank l'chaim. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [SPEAKING YIDDISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '25, 1925, 13-- 13 years. And 13 years and one day, you got the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after your bar mitzvah? It was 1925. What did you do after? How did you keep yourself busy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for [INAUDIBLE]. I helped my parents to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till what age? Then I was-- in 1936, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and '37. I opened up-- as a young boy, I opened up a store-- a hardware store, because that time was very bad for the kosher butcher stores. Was a lady in the government from Poland by the name Mrs. Pristor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how to spell her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Pristor. P-- I'll-- I'll-- you want [INAUDIBLE]? Pristor, P-R-I-S-T-O-R. Pristor. She was in the-- in the senate. And she was pushing to forbid the Jewish people to kill for kosher. It was very anti-Semitic. And she passed a law for Jewish people so much. And that's it. [INAUDIBLE] kosher meat, you couldn't have trafe together. It was a [INAUDIBLE] small town, was four kosher butchers. Then they had to close up, because only one was kosher. And this was a-- a problem, a big, big problem. It was bad already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there enough meat for the population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: If there-- was there enough meat available?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a lot of times, was very, very short. But they couldn't help it. They covered up with poultry. Poultry was not included. Only cows, lambs, calves, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of hobbies did you have, and what kind of newspapers did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was reading the Jewish newspapers, the-- the [INAUDIBLE], then there was [INAUDIBLE], you know, and another couple. But I was reading the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for entertainment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked like a horse. That was my hobby in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your hardware store. How-- Tell me about your hardware store. How did you come to open it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- [LAUGHS] it's a good question. I was not thinking good. To start it good, before I'm doing. But I went in blind, and I did it. And I succeeded. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I took a chance. It was good. Till the Germans came in, and they took away everything for the-- the Polish people come in. I was forced to open up the store, and they [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have in the hardware store?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had all kinds of hardware, for-- for horse and wagons, for the wagons, material. And for houses, locks, for windows the hinges. And a lot of things. Nail. And-- it's very hard to explain everything, but I-- like in a small town, the hardware like in any other small towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only one working there? Did you have other people working?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was another store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you had somebody else working with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brother's daughter, Esther Silverman. She was working with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old a woman was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in the 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many days was the store open?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war? Five days. Saturday was closed, and Sunday was not allowed to open up by the Polish law.

speaker: interviewer
text: So till when did you have the hardware store, till what year? Till what year did you have the hardware store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till '39. In September, the war came in, and took [? more than ?] two weeks, and the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something about the Polish reserves? Were you in the Polish reserves?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the-- on the waiting list to be called in. But they-- before they looked around, it was already after the war. The Germans arrived. I mean, after the-- after the Polish army was fighting, it was finished. It took two weeks, too, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it come about that they took you for the reserves? What was the purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: The purpose was I had at that time-- I was-- went through a sickness or something. I don't remember what kind. Not such a serious, but they took me in-- [? we'll take him ?] in reserve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What-- what did-- what did you do in the reserves? Did you have to practice something? Did you have to go for training? If they-- if they would call me in, they would train me to use weapons. But unfortunately, it was already-- two weeks was finished. The Polish army couldn't-- couldn't do anything. They gave up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the year it was that you were in the reserve?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go in. They didn't call me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the reserves--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what year were you in the reserves?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was in 1933. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To what organizations did you belong?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a-- a few organizations. I belonged to the [INAUDIBLE]. And that was a lot of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like in-- like in every small town in Europe, it was [INAUDIBLE]. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me just remind myself. There was the-- like in any other state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father proud of you that you made the success with the hardware store?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very proud of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your other brothers and sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the meat business. And they-- they struggled very bad, because account of the-- of the non-kosher law came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, what was the political situation in the late 1930s? Was there any talk about Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Polish-- maybe not every, but a lot of them, they already mentioned Hitler's name. Hitler's going to come on you. When he has something, Hitler's gonna come. And we started to suffer a lot already when Hitler-- when Hitler became in power.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you suffer? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish governments kept together with a relationship. And anti-Semitic was jumping, growing from the ground, in moments everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened at that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to reload the tape, and then we will--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we will talk further about the political situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't press me too much politically. I was not. OK.
